source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: DRAM companies are deeply undervalued relative to semi-cap equipment companies, and Baker knows of no one else who is bearish on DRAM — a sign of dangerous consensus on semi-cap
summary_extended: Baker argues that DRAM companies are deeply undervalued relative to semi-cap equipment companies, and that the universal bullishness on DRAM represents a dangerous consensus that the market has not yet recognized as a risk. He explicitly states he knows of no one who shares his skepticism on DRAM, framing this near-unanimous bullishness as a contrarian signal that the DRAM trade is crowded and semi-cap equipment valuations may be stretched relative to the underlying memory producers. The investment implication is a relative value trade: DRAM producers offer better risk/reward than semi-cap equipment names, and the valuation gap between the two is extreme and unsustainable. Baker also separately highlights Astera Labs (ALAB) as a high-conviction long he has held since Series C, citing its quality and the presence of bears as characteristics he finds attractive in a position. The two ideas — DRAM vs. semi-cap and ALAB — appear in the same segment but represent distinct theses.
detail: Baker explicitly states he knows of no one who is not bullish on DRAM. He frames the valuation gap between semi-cap equipment and DRAM as extreme and unsustainable. The implication is DRAM is the better risk/reward. He has been long Astera Labs for a long time (since Series C) and views it as a high-quality name with bears, which he loves.
confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly states 'I don't know of anyone like me who's not really bullish on DRAM' and provides specific valuation comparisons to support the relative trade
quote_fragment: I don't know of anyone who's not really bullish on DRAM
